GUN
OWNERS
OF CALIFORNIA

CAMPAIGN COMMITTEE

3440 VIKING DRIVE, SUITE 106 • SACRAMENTO, CA 95827
(916) 361·3109

May 10, 1990

Mr. John H. Larson, Chairman
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, #800
Sacramento, CA. 95814

Dear Mr. Larson:

With only a few short weeks left until the June
primary, I would call your attention to a number of
concerns we have regarding the 76th Assembly District
and what can or cannot be done by special interest
groups to support candidates.

We have been reviewing the campaign committees that
are sponsored by and supporting Tricia Hunter in the
76th Assembly District. There are currently two
committees: Committee to Elect Tricia Hunter
(C.T.E.T.H.) #890811 and Friends of Tricia Hunter
(F.T.H.) #892171. This has caused considerable
confusion and I would appreciate the following
clarifications:

Can the second committee be used to avoid repayment
of debts from a previous campaign? Tricia was left
with almost $73,000 in debt on the 12-31-89 report
for C.T.E.T.H. As of 3-17-90 over $28,000 had been
raised and only $6,369 applied to the debt. If she
is not planning to retire the debt prior to June 5th,
does it then become a contribution and is it subject
to any limitations?

The use of two committees and three reporting periods
has also made it very difficult to determine who has
or has not exceeded the campaign contribution limits.
It would be helpful in campaigns to provide some sort
of consolidated reporting.

A Political Committee dedicated to crime control not gun control.

In the meantime, we have found the following which appear to
exceed legal limits, even considering the three periods:

1) The California Trial Lawyers show a fiscal year total of
$10,000 on the 12-31-89 report for C.T.E.T.H. and an
additional amount of $5,500 shown on the F.T.H. report of 12-
31-89. This appears to put them $500 over the limit and an
additional $1,000 contribution is shown on the 3-17-90 F.T.H.
report.

2) A Dr. McGargrigle or Mcgarrigle is listed for
contributions of $4,000 between the two committees and this
appears to exceed his $3,000 limit.

3) On 8-22-90, the Californians of Individual Rights &
Liberties contributed $1,050. They do not seem to be
registered as a PAC. Would they have a $1,000 limit for a
single contribution?

Mr. Larson, this is a very close race for the Republican
primary in this district and it is critically important that
these matters be resolved as soon as possible. Should the
Hunter campaign be required to immediately return any
contributions that exceed the legal limits? Would this also
apply to retirement of past debts before their cash on
hand is depleted with current campaign activities?

In addition, I have some questions about other special
interest expenditures and actions. In going through the
reports, it appears that both the Abortion Rights Action
League and the California Nurses Association have been very
active in soliciting contributions. Are they each under the
$15,000 limit and how do we tell which of their chapters are
giving as independent units and what is a coordinated state
wide effort? They have also made substantial "independent
expenditures" on behalf of Hunter.

Would the current regulations allow Gun Owners to set up a
series of local chapters as PAC's and then have each PAC
contribute the maximum limit under the law to the candidates
of their chose? This would be a very effective way for us to
organize the gun owners, but my understanding was that
current law did not allow this type of coordinated activity.

I am also confused about what reporting requirements a group making independent expenditure has. When I see nurses mailing on the gun issue (see enclosed mailer #1), I have to ask myself, where did they get the information, to what list did they mail and who provided it?

I have also enclosed a spring mailer from the nurses and the response devise and envelope would seem to indicate they are working directly with the campaign. However, the disclaimers still seem to indicate they are treating this as an independent expenditure. Are they now under the spending limits?

I have enclosed a third mailer sent to doctors and it does not appear as an in-kind contribution on any of the 3-27-90 reports. Again, am I to understand that Gun Owners would face no reporting requirements or dollar limitations if we simply fund-raise for candidates we support?

I understand that time is short on this election cycle, but I believe that immediate and effective action should be taken by your commission and staff to resolve the issues I have raised. I will look forward to your prompt response.

Sincerely,

[signature]

SENATOR H.L. RICHARDSON (Ret.)

Encs.

#2

[LOGO: California Nurses Association Political Action Committee]

CALIFORNIA
NURSES
ASSOCIATION

POLITICAL
ACTION
COMMITTEE


Dear Nurse Colleague:

On October 10, 1989, Tricia Hunter, RN, was sworn in as a Member of the California State Assembly, representing the 76th Assembly District. OUR DREAM HAS TURNED TO REALITY—WE DID IT!!!

There is no question that without the support of the California Nurses Association, its members and other nurses who gave money, time, energy and every kind of support imaginable, the outcome of the race would have been much different. Tricia won the election—by 156 votes!

Our initial investment in Tricia Hunter has paid off. Now it's time to demonstrate our continued support and confidence in Assemblywoman Hunter. Even though Tricia was elected just six months ago, she must now stand for re-election, along with every member of the State Assembly.

Once again Tricia is facing major opposition in the June primary election which is financed by a well-organized ultraright-wing political faction. These people have even had the gall to find a registered nurse who espouses their extremist views to challenge Tricia. Perhaps they think that having two nurses run against each other will split the nursing support which was so critical to Tricia's successful campaign. We don't think so.

Tricia Hunter has been an active member of CNA for more than fifteen years. She served as Chair of the Government Relations Commission and Vice President of the CNA Board of Directors. She is immediate past President of the Board of Registered Nursing and an active member of the Association of Operating Room Nurses.

Tricia has been an advocate for nurses in the political arena for a long time—and she will continue to speak out on nursing issues as a State Legislator. Her challenger, Connie Youngkin, has no active record on issues of crucial importance to California nurses. Have you ever heard her name mentioned in connection with nursing issues?

When Tricia first ran for the Assembly, she was proud to run as nurse and emphasized her professional identity in all her campaign literature. Assemblywoman Hunter has always advocated on behalf of our professional issues. She has a proven record of leadership. While president of the Board of Registered Nursing, she chaired the

This mailing is not authorized or approved by any legislative candidate or election official. It is paid for by the California Nurses Association/Political Action Committee, 1855 Folsom Street, Suite 670, San Francisco, CA 94103

CNA PAC
1855 Folsom Street, Suite 670
San Francisco, CA 94103

important Nursing Shortage Committee. She has also worked hard to promote the workplace and social issues
which make up an important part of the CNA agenda. Issues such as:

• Universal health care coverage
• Reproductive freedom
• Comparable worth
• Collective bargaining rights for nurses
• Support for anti-discrimination provisions based on gender, race,
  or sexual preference

Her challenger, Connie Youngkin, is strongly anti-choice, and a leader in the effort to close down family
planning clinics. In a recent news article she was quoted as saying that she would fire any legislative staffers who
she found out were homosexual, or otherwise functioned "against Scripture."

Which nurse do you want to speak for nursing as an elected member of the State Assembly, Tricia Hunter
or Connie Youngkin?

Assemblywoman Tricia Hunter needs our help now, as much as before. If we want to send a message
about CNA's support for NURSING, and all that NURSING stands for—if we want to keep Tricia Hunter in office
—then we need to send our message NOW.

In order to defend against her opposition, Tricia must raise us much money as she did last year. And
because this election is won or lost in the primary on June 5th, she must do it RIGHT AWAY.

Once again, we're asking that you put your money where your heart is—in nursing. Let's come
through for Tricia—and for ourselves—by contributing as much as we can. PLEASE DO IT TODAY.
WE DON'T HAVE A MINUTE TO WASTE!

Sincerely,

Mary Foley                                    Jeanette Dillman
Mary Foley, RN                            Jeanette Dillman, RN
President, CNA                           Chair, CNA-PAC Board of Trustees

This mailing is not authorized or approved by any legislative candidate or election official. It is paid for by the
California Nurses Association/Political Action Committee, 1855 Folsom Street, Suite 670, San Francisco, CA 94103

CALLICERATINA
EUTRICATES
ASSOCIATION
COMMITTEE

ERTCA

9400 E. 4th St.
National City, CA 92050
Phone: 470-6111
Ext 2150

PARADISE VALLEY HOSPITAL
RADIOLOGY MEDICAL GROUP
T G Braun M.D., Med Corp
J F Healy, M.D., Med Corp
J D Traxtral, M.D. Med Corp

March 5, 1990

Dear Colleague:

The volume of legislation in Sacramento affecting physicians and our patients grows with
each legislative session. The range of issues includes virtually every facet of our
professional lives.

It is imperative that we have elected officials who truly understand the complexities of
health care delivery. For the past ten years only one legislator, Assemblyman Bill
Filante, a practicing ophthalmologist, has served as the resident state assembly expert on
health and medical issues.

With the selection of Tricia Hunter to the assembly, we now have another health care
professional who is a knowledgeable and effective advocate for practicing physicians. As
a nurse for 15 years, a director of surgical services at Chula Vista Community Hospital
and a long-time activist on health care issues, she has seen first-hand how uninformed
legislators and bureaucrats have enacted poorly thought out and unnecessary regulations
and statutes affecting our profession.

Accordingly, we would like to invite you to meet Tricia and her special guest, Dr. Ken
Kizer, director of the Department of Health Services for the State of California. Dr.
Kizer will be the luncheon guest speaker at a special fundraising event to be held at the
San Diego County Medical Society, March 30, 1990. The event will begin at 11:30 a.m. and
conclude at 1:30 p.m.

The entire event will be devoted to discussing health care issues and how Tricia can help
solve the critical problems we face as practicing physicians. Complaining about the state
of our profession in hospital lunch rooms accomplishes nothing.

This is an opportunity to communicate with an important legislator and an administrative
official who can do something about it.

Please fill out the enclosed envelope and mail it along with your $250 check made payable
to FRIENDS OF TRICIA HUNTER.

We look forward to seeing you!

Sincerely,

[illegible] M.D.
Jacquelin Traxtral, M.D.

[illegible] M.D.
Gledden V. Elliott, M.D.

Bruce J. Tariff, M.D.
[illegible] M.D.
Robert E. Hertzka, M.D.

JDT:GVE:BJT:REH:esc

Enclosure

P.S. As a result of last year's expensive special election, Tricia has a personal debt
of $90,000. CALPAC has already given her the maximum under the law. Now it is up to you
to help her directly.
